texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Recovery of Freon Gases with Silicone Rubber Membranes
texts.text: Recovery of Freon Gases with Silicone Rubber Membranes

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Daryl L. Roberts* and Gordon D. Chlngt
texts.text: Daryl L. Roberts* and Gordon D. Chlngt

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chemical Engineering Laboratory, SRI International, Menlo Park, California 94025
texts.text: Chemical Engineering Laboratory, SRI International, Menlo Park, California 94025

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability through silicone rubber membranes has been measured for a variety of Freon gases (CF4, CCIF3, CCI2F2, CHCIFj, and C2CIF5). These gases are common solvents In various Industries, and the possibility exists to recover these solvents by permeation through a suitably selective membrane. We found that the permeation of Freons could be correlated rather simply on a plot of permeability vs. selectivity (with respect to oxygen). The permeability of CF4 was found to be 15 times less than that of oxygen. This observation poses the possibility of recovering CF4 from the exhaust of plasma etching reactors commonly used in semiconductor manufacturing. In an example design calculation, we found that CF4 could readily be recovered In a purity of 99.9-99.99%. To process 500 cm3 (STP)/mln of reactor exhaust gas, the membrane unit would require only about 50 ft2 of membrane surface area. Although only one-third to one-half of the CF4 would be recovered, this system could pay for itself in about 200 h of operation.
texts.text: The permeability through silicone rubber membranes has been measured for a variety of Freon gases (CF4, CCIF3, CCI2F2, CHCIFj, and C2CIF5). These gases are common solvents In various Industries, and the possibility exists to recover these solvents by permeation through a suitably selective membrane. We found that the permeation of Freons could be correlated rather simply on a plot of permeability vs. selectivity (with respect to oxygen). The permeability of CF4 was found to be 15 times less than that of oxygen. This observation poses the possibility of recovering CF4 from the exhaust of plasma etching reactors commonly used in semiconductor manufacturing. In an example design calculation, we found that CF4 could readily be recovered In a purity of 99.9-99.99%. To process 500 cm3 (STP)/mln of reactor exhaust gas, the membrane unit would require only about 50 ft2 of membrane surface area. Although only one-third to one-half of the CF4 would be recovered, this system could pay for itself in about 200 h of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation of gases and vapors is gaining substantial industrial acceptance as a unit operation. Recent commercial successes include the preparation of inert gas from air (McReynolds, 1985) and the recovery of C02 at wells undergoing C02 flooding for enhanced oil recovery (Detloff and Cooley, 1985). The success of the Monsanto PRISM separators is well-known (e.g., MacLean et al., 1980) and second generation membrane materials are actively being pursued (e.g., Zampini and Malón, 1985).
texts.text: Membrane separation of gases and vapors is gaining substantial industrial acceptance as a unit operation. Recent commercial successes include the preparation of inert gas from air (McReynolds, 1985) and the recovery of C02 at wells undergoing C02 flooding for enhanced oil recovery (Detloff and Cooley, 1985). The success of the Monsanto PRISM separators is well-known (e.g., MacLean et al., 1980) and second generation membrane materials are actively being pursued (e.g., Zampini and Malón, 1985).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A potentially significant, developing application for membranes is in the recovery of solvents. Permeation behavior of a number of industrial solvent vapors has been studied by Baker et al. (1984,1985). These techniques may have widespread use in the dry cleaning and automotive industries.
texts.text: A potentially significant, developing application for membranes is in the recovery of solvents. Permeation behavior of a number of industrial solvent vapors has been studied by Baker et al. (1984,1985). These techniques may have widespread use in the dry cleaning and automotive industries.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another potential application of membranes for solvent recovery lies in the semiconductor business where large quantities of Freon gases are employed for cleaning disk heads, for making plasma etch mixtures, and the like. In most industrial situations, these solvents are either discarded after use or adsorbed onto carbon beds which are regenerated off-site by vendors. With stricter EPA policies on solvent emissions and the need to reduce production costs in these industries, there is a growing incentive for solvent recovery.
texts.text: Another potential application of membranes for solvent recovery lies in the semiconductor business where large quantities of Freon gases are employed for cleaning disk heads, for making plasma etch mixtures, and the like. In most industrial situations, these solvents are either discarded after use or adsorbed onto carbon beds which are regenerated off-site by vendors. With stricter EPA policies on solvent emissions and the need to reduce production costs in these industries, there is a growing incentive for solvent recovery.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our laboratory we have measured the permeability of silicone rubber membranes to a variety of Freon gases. With these data we have posed a scheme whereby a Freon gas commonly used in plasma etching (Freon 14, CF4) can be recovered and recycled. We have not dealt with some of the practical problems that a company in the manufacturing business may face in implementing a membrane recovery unit into its operations. However, we believe that our data and analysis will serve as a starting point for those who are concerned with reducing solvent losses in these and other industries employing Freon gases.
texts.text: In our laboratory we have measured the permeability of silicone rubber membranes to a variety of Freon gases. With these data we have posed a scheme whereby a Freon gas commonly used in plasma etching (Freon 14, CF4) can be recovered and recycled. We have not dealt with some of the practical problems that a company in the manufacturing business may face in implementing a membrane recovery unit into its operations. However, we believe that our data and analysis will serve as a starting point for those who are concerned with reducing solvent losses in these and other industries employing Freon gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane utilized in these experiments was silicone rubber on a polysulfone backing. The membrane was prepared by Membrane Technology and Research, Inc. (Menlo Park, CA). The membrane was nominally 1 µ thick, although our oxygen permeation data indicated a thickness of about 0.7 pm when compared to published data on oxygen permeability through silicone rubber (Robb, 1968).
texts.text: The membrane utilized in these experiments was silicone rubber on a polysulfone backing. The membrane was prepared by Membrane Technology and Research, Inc. (Menlo Park, CA). The membrane was nominally 1 µ thick, although our oxygen permeation data indicated a thickness of about 0.7 pm when compared to published data on oxygen permeability through silicone rubber (Robb, 1968).

texts.orig: The cell holds a membrane that is 14 cm wide and 19 cm long. The section of the membrane actually exposed to gases and áctive in the permeation process is 11.4 cm by 16.5 cm (188.7 cm2 of area). The flow of feed and sweep gases was countercurrent to minimize the effect of the change in composition of the feed gas and the sweep gas that occurs as these gases flow over the membrane. When the permeabilities were computed, the partial pressure driving force was taken to be the arithmetic averages of the inlet and outlet values. In no case did these values differ by more than 10

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1. Test bed for membrane permeation measurements. The polymeric membrane is held in the cell. The gas of interest (e.g., Freon) is delivered at a known flow rate to one side of the cell. A clean helium stream sweeps away any feed gas that passes through the film. Gas chromatography (thermal conductivity detector) is used to determine the concentration of feed gas in the sweep stream (WTM = wet test meter).
texts.text: Figure 1. Test bed for membrane permeation measurements. The polymeric membrane is held in the cell. The gas of interest (e.g., Freon) is delivered at a known flow rate to one side of the cell. A clean helium stream sweeps away any feed gas that passes through the film. Gas chromatography (thermal conductivity detector) is used to determine the concentration of feed gas in the sweep stream (WTM = wet test meter).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 2. Cell for holding polymeric or immobilized liquid membranes. Gases flow along membrane through thin chamber cut into Plexiglas spacer. The screen reduces gas-phase resistance to mass transfer. Countercurrent or cocurrent flow of the feed and sweep stream is possible.

texts.orig: The transmembrane flux, normalized by the partial pressure driving force, is given for each gas in Table I. In these tests the feed gas and sweep gas pressure was 76 cmHg, and the temperature was 23 °C. Using the permeability of oxygen through silicone rubber given by Robb (1968), we deduce that the membrane is approximately 0.69 µ thick. With this thickness, we can compute membrane permeabilities (Table II). The selectivity of the membrane using oxygen as the reference gas is also listed in Table II.
texts.text: The transmembrane flux, normalized by the partial pressure driving force, is given for each gas in Table I. In these tests the feed gas and sweep gas pressure was 76 cmHg, and the temperature was 23 °C. Using the permeability of oxygen through silicone rubber given by Robb (1968), we deduce that the membrane is approximately 0.69 µ thick. With this thickness, we can compute membrane permeabilities (Table II). The selectivity of the membrane using oxygen as the reference gas is also listed in Table II.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Possible Applications in Semiconductor Manufacturing. The permeation ratios of many of the Freon gases in silicone rubber are not sufficiently different from air to permit economical recovery of the Freon gases. Indeed, other membrane materials may provide more favorable selectivities. However, with silicone rubber we can speculate on the basis of Figure 3 that Freon 11 (CC13F) would permeate rather readily through silicone rubber and therefore be recoverable in instances where Freon 11 is used as a wash solvent. Also, hydrogen-containing Freons could be separated from similar Freons that have a chlorine or fluorine substituted in place of the hydrogen (in our case Freon 22 from Freon 12).
texts.text: Possible Applications in Semiconductor Manufacturing. The permeation ratios of many of the Freon gases in silicone rubber are not sufficiently different from air to permit economical recovery of the Freon gases. Indeed, other membrane materials may provide more favorable selectivities. However, with silicone rubber we can speculate on the basis of Figure 3 that Freon 11 (CC13F) would permeate rather readily through silicone rubber and therefore be recoverable in instances where Freon 11 is used as a wash solvent. Also, hydrogen-containing Freons could be separated from similar Freons that have a chlorine or fluorine substituted in place of the hydrogen (in our case Freon 22 from Freon 12).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With our results to date, however, the best application in semiconductor manufacturing appears to be the recovery of CF4 from the exhaust gas of a plasma etching reactor, one that utilizes a feed gas of CF4 mixed with 02. From the standpoint of Freon recovery, a plasma etching reactor is a container into which a feed gas (typically 96% CF4, 4% 02) is drawn at low pressure [see Sze (1983) for discussion of plasma reactors]. The feed gas flows over the device being etched, and a portion is ionized. Very little of the feed gas is consumed in the reactor. Therefore, the exhaust, which is pumped out of the reactor, has approx-
texts.text: With our results to date, however, the best application in semiconductor manufacturing appears to be the recovery of CF4 from the exhaust gas of a plasma etching reactor, one that utilizes a feed gas of CF4 mixed with 02. From the standpoint of Freon recovery, a plasma etching reactor is a container into which a feed gas (typically 96% CF4, 4% 02) is drawn at low pressure [see Sze (1983) for discussion of plasma reactors]. The feed gas flows over the device being etched, and a portion is ionized. Very little of the feed gas is consumed in the reactor. texts.orig: imately the same composition as the feed gas, but it contains a wide variety of impurities from the plasma reactions (e.g., COF2 and C0C12,F2). Although we are not certain how all of these impurities will behave in a silicone rubber permeation device, we would expect that these impurities would permeate with oxygen because of their polarity and reactivity with respect to CF4. At this point, we can at least be assured of removing the oxygen from the CF4, producing purified CF4.

At this point, we can at least be assured of removing the oxygen from the CF4, producing purified CF4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flow diagram for membrane recovery of CF4 can be depicted as in Figure 4. The first vacuum pump is required by the plasma reactor even without the membrane unit. Consequently, the additional pieces of equipment to recover the CF4 are the membrane unit and the second vacuum pump.
texts.text: The flow diagram for membrane recovery of CF4 can be depicted as in Figure 4. The first vacuum pump is required by the plasma reactor even without the membrane unit. Consequently, the additional pieces of equipment to recover the CF4 are the membrane unit and the second vacuum pump.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have used the design technique outlined by Pan and Habgood (1974) and the permeability and selectivity values from our data to calculate the performance of a spiralwound silicone rubber membrane module in this application. As an example, we assume that the exhaust of the first vacuum pump is at 760 mmHg and the permeate side of the membrane is held at 76 mmHg. We also assume that the membrane is 25 µ thick because films this thin are commercially available (e.g., General Electric Co.). The design technique of Pan and Habgood assumes the permeability of each gas is independent of the partial pressures of the gas components in the feed and permeate streams. This condition is nearly certain to be fulfilled in the instance of 02 and CF4 in silicone rubber at partial pressures below 3400 kPa (see, for example, Epperson and Burnett, 1983).
texts.text: We have used the design technique outlined by Pan and Habgood (1974) and the permeability and selectivity values from our data to calculate the performance of a spiralwound silicone rubber membrane module in this application. As an example, we assume that the exhaust of the first vacuum pump is at 760 mmHg and the permeate side of the membrane is held at 76 mmHg. We also assume that the membrane is 25 µ thick because films this thin are commercially available (e.g., General Electric Co.). The design technique of Pan and Habgood assumes the permeability of each gas is independent of the partial pressures of the gas components in the feed and permeate streams. This condition is nearly certain to be fulfilled in the instance of 02 and CF4 in silicone rubber at partial pressures below 3400 kPa (see, for example, Epperson and Burnett, 1983).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The economic payback period of such a membrane system would be less than 2 months. A typical membrane unit would cost approximately $2/ft2 of membrane area, and at /kwh of electricity, the operating costs would be less than $2/year. With a small vacuum pump capable of drawing a vacuum to 76 mmHg absolute, the membrane unit with vacuum pump could be installed for less than $500. Since a typical cost of Freon 14 is $5/mol (more when purchased as a 96% CF4/4% 02 standard), the unit would pay for itself in about 200 h of operation.
texts.text: The economic payback period of such a membrane system would be less than 2 months. A typical membrane unit would cost approximately $2/ft2 of membrane area, and at /kwh of electricity, the operating costs would be less than $2/year. With a small vacuum pump capable of drawing a vacuum to 76 mmHg absolute, the membrane unit with vacuum pump could be installed for less than $500. Since a typical cost of Freon 14 is $5/mol (more when purchased as a 96% CF4/4% 02 standard), the unit would pay for itself in about 200 h of operation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Discussion and Conclusions
texts.text: Discussion and Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of solvents through membrane films may pose a new technology for recovery of Freon gases in a number of industries, including semiconductor manufacturing. Solvent recovery not only would save money in many instances but would reduce dependence on outside vendors and provide easier compliance with EPA regulations.
texts.text: The permeation of solvents through membrane films may pose a new technology for recovery of Freon gases in a number of industries, including semiconductor manufacturing. Solvent recovery not only would save money in many instances but would reduce dependence on outside vendors and provide easier compliance with EPA regulations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our laboratories we have found that the permeability of a variety of Freon gases through silicon rubber films can be correlated rather simply. Some of these gases permeate much more slowly than air, posing the opportunity to remove the air from the Freon gas, thereby purifying the Freon gas for recycle. The best example in the data we have taken to date is that of CF4 which permeates 15 times more slowly than 02.
texts.text: In our laboratories we have found that the permeability of a variety of Freon gases through silicon rubber films can be correlated rather simply. Some of these gases permeate much more slowly than air, posing the opportunity to remove the air from the Freon gas, thereby purifying the Freon gas for recycle. The best example in the data we have taken to date is that of CF4 which permeates 15 times more slowly than 02.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the basis of this observation, we have performed design calculations for a membrane device for the purification of CF4 in the exhaust of a plasma etching reactor. This device could recover 33-50% of the CF4 with a purity of 99.9-99.99%. We believe that the short calculated payback period justifies the exploration of this technique to see if practical issues, such as the fate of trace impurities, could be overcome so that the door could be open to implementation of Freon solvent recovery in actual manufacturing facilities.
texts.text: On the basis of this observation, we have performed design calculations for a membrane device for the purification of CF4 in the exhaust of a plasma etching reactor. This device could recover 33-50% of the CF4 with a purity of 99.9-99.99%. We believe that the short calculated payback period justifies the exploration of this technique to see if practical issues, such as the fate of trace impurities, could be overcome so that the door could be open to implementation of Freon solvent recovery in actual manufacturing facilities.
